_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve depicts the yields of Treasury securities across different maturities, typically from 1 month to 30 years. An upward sloping curve is considered normal and indicates higher yields for longer maturities to compensate for risks over time. An inverted curve, where short-term yields exceed long-term yields, has historically preceded every modern US recession, generally by 6 to 18 months. Currently, the 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.6% (maturing Jun 2026), and the 2-year to 10-year spread (10Y-2Y) is positive at 0.37 points, reflecting a re-steepening from the deepest inversion of 0 points experienced during 2022-2024.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread serves as a classic recession indicator, with inversions historically signaling elevated recession risk. The fact that the curve spent 2022-2024 inverted but has since moved back above zero indicates a normalization of market expectations or reduced immediate recession probability, though un-inversion often occurs shortly before recessions. The New York Fed currently estimates a 31% probability of recession, implying material but not certain risk. This combination of data suggests caution but not an imminent economic contraction based solely on the yield curve dynamics.
notes.now: In sum, the yield curve has transitioned from an inverted state to a mildly positive slope, signaling that recession risks may have moderated but remain present. The current 31% recession probability corroborates a moderate risk environment without definitive timing. Investors and analysts should consider this nuanced signal alongside other economic indicators for a comprehensive assessment.
